abstract.text: This article describes the essential parts of KeyboardKit.

inlineContent.text: This article describes the essential parts of KeyboardKit. See the
guide for information on how to set up KeyboardKit for your main app and keyboard extension.
, which is such a central part of the SDK that it has a separate article.
👑 KeyboardKit Pro can be used to unlock additional features based on the
unlocks all locales and most features.
KeyboardKit uses namespaces to group types into features, like
, etc. This helps making the library surface smaller and easier to navigate.
namespace contains many essential types and views. By typing “Keyboard.”, Xcode will list all types in this namespace, like
Each namespace has surface level types that are more important. For instance, the
, and other essential types that are placed at the surface level for the sake of discoverability.

guide on how to inherit it to get a additional

Most features have a settings type like this. You can access them through the feature-specific contexts, using the
property from each context. All settings share the same
, which can sync data between the app and its keyboard.

namespace has many views, styles, and view-related types that make it easy to build great keyboards, like a keyboard
mimics a native system keyboard. It can be used for many different
s, supports all supported locales, layouts, callouts, etc.
This component is so important that it has a dedicated
article that describes how to use it.
is enabled, to show a compact toolbar.
has a collapsed content builder that can let you specify which view to use as the collapsed content.
KeyboardKit Pro automatically detects when an external keyboard is connected, and update this state accordingly.
mimics a native number pad, which can be used for pure numeric inputs.

applies a minimum height to its content.
always renders a toolbar by default, to avoid callouts from being cut off.
to remove the toolbar, but it’s not recommended, since a custom keyboard can’t render content outside of its frame.

to customize the autocomplete toolbar style, and
capabilities, including full support for all
-related settings views that make it easier to manage settings in the main app, or in the keyboard:
can handle keyboard settings in an app or keyboard extension.
The screen lists various settings, grouped by type. All changes are automatically persisted and synced to the keyboard.
This screen can be customized with the following view modifiers:
can handle locale and language settings in an app or keyboard extension.
The screen lists all added and all available locales, and lets users add and reorganize locales, change keyboard layouts, etc.
This screen can be customized with the following view modifiers:
-related views and utilities, that make it easier to create even more powerful keyboards:
renders a bottom row with the same behavior as a full
The bottom row view can be used to provide basic control, while providing a different main view.
This view can be styled with
KeyboardKit Pro will automatically add an additional input toolbar above
on large iPads, to fit the native keyboard.
You can also add an additional input bar to any device and locale,
modifier to customize the toolbar and its buttons.
can be used to toggle between two toolbars, e.g. to place a main menu “behind” the autocomplete toolbar.
This view can be styled with
guide has information on how you can replace or customize the standard
with a few lines of code.
You can also render custom views just like you would any of the unsupported keyboard types, as demonstrated in the previous section.

abstract.text: This view renders a view that mimics the native keyboard.
